makes this big promise and then talks about the pain of avoiding

really inspires your child or employee or dog? You might be 	surprised

How do you inspire the uninspired? What if the target of our

to do? What if the dog just wants to be left alone or maybe 	the dog
already has a history of his own inspiration somewhere 	long way,
way away from you?
What if our employees really just want to do the basics or the

options.

This myth busting really is great, because if you can go in and take

Susan Garrett: 	I’m not saying that rewards to animals can’t have an impact on
behavior. My suggestion is that truly the best rewards are those 	that
create the longest lasting and the biggest impacts on behavior 	come
as a result of giving the animal the opportunity to choose, 	the power of
choice, choice to create their own outcome.
Since consequences are a powerful driving force, nothing can
compete with the reinforcement value received when an animal’s 	choice
produces the consequences of a highly desired positive 	outcome.
Jeff Walker: 	In this next sections Susan has lots and lots of teaching for the
next about three and a half minutes. I’m going to skip forward 	again.
I encourage you to watch how she does this. It’s just a 	wonderful

Susan Garrett: 	Inspired training system, you’re creating an addiction of sorts for
that animal wanting to participate in any activity that you ask. Let 	me
give you an example of this in my own life.
My oldest border collie named Buzz, when he was younger, he was
crazy about swimming, obsessed you might say. The first time I 	ever
let him in the pond, he swam, I timed him, he swam for 30 	minutes
without giving thought to coming out.
His love for swimming became a problem so I had to put some
criteria on it. He wasn’t allowed to swim unless I gave him the cue, go for
a swim. Buzz and I ended up with this game. He would just 	stand by the
edge of the pool or a pond and he would stare at it, 	look at me and
stare at me and wait for that cue. Sometimes I 	gave it to him and
sometimes I didn’t.
Jeff Walker: 	Susan continues on with the story here. It’s really powerful to bring
a story into your prelaunch content. Then she continues on lots and
lots of teaching, really high value teaching throughout here
continues to go on.

In this section, up around 12 and a half minutes, she starts to get
very specific. In here, there’s the two choices so there’s this theme
throughout this video of the two different ways to go, two different
choices. Up around 12:50, she starts to get very specific about
strategies and tactics that you can use in your training. This is very high
value stuff. Let me just give you a quick example.
Susan Garrett: 	If we were like good parents we would decide to restrict this
puppy’s access to all these amazing environmental reinforcements. We
would house a puppy in a size appropriate crate anytime we couldn’t
supervise his activities.
We would use a larger exercise pen but only if we were in the same
room that we could see what was going on. Anytime the puppy
would be with adult dogs, we’d supervise that interaction and
frequently interrupt it with short bits playing with us so that the 	puppy
learns that all good things, all of those reinforcing choices 	come from
working with people first.
We play games to create value for people and always have the
puppy on a leash anytime we’re outside in the garden. When it 	comes
time to want to teach the puppy …
Jeff Walker: 	Through this next section, Susan just keeps on giving very specific

proof and great content. She does that throughout this entire 	video.

everybody?
I’m going to leave you with that thought. It may seem like an
